content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Tarantino really boxed himself in by deciding to retire after making 10 movies. He knows he has to make his last film a memorable one. That’s why he cancelled The Movie Critic, and why it’s taking so much time.

**Score:** 617

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He did box himself in a little but it’s not like he’s George RR Martin and Game of Thrones. If Tarantino wants more time to make his final movie that’s fine with me. People waited 16 years for George Lucas to make another movie between Return of the Jedi and Phantom Menace
  - 16 years to come up with Jar Jar Binks 😅
    - To be fair he was created to engage with children. I was a child back then. And I can confidently tell you it worked.
2. He doesn't have to follow through with his 10 movie limit and I doubt he will. He is a movie lover first, I bet a year goes by before he starts writing another script after his 10th
  - He is having fun now. 

He is writing scripts and is thinking about limited series and other forms of entertainment like theatre.

He is also a great guest on shows and podcasts. He knows how to keep the listeners entertained with his stories and ideas. 

I don't need a new Tarantino movie just for the sake of it. Let the man cook. 

What I need is more stories about behind the scenes and his knowledge about cinema.
    - Well said.  I could listen to him talk all day about the art of cinema and script writing.
3. He may not direct after his 10th movie, but I believe he’ll write a few more after that.
4. He doesn’t have to…he can just change his mind. People who don’t like Tarantino won’t care much either way, people who do like him will be jazzed they get more movies.
5. At worst he can continue under the pseudonym Tintin Quarantino or something.
6. I'd rather see him take his time then rush. Let him cook.

## 2. A movie that you wish you could erase from your memory so you can watch it again for the first time.

**Score:** 321

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Matrix
2. Interstellar was such an impressive movie to see in theaters, wish I could go back to watch it again for the first time.
3. Lawrence of Arabia.

Only 3 hour + movie that upon completion I immediately started it over watched it entirely again. Not because I missed parts, or there was a big reveal that made ya want to watch it again.

No.

Simply because it was the greatest film I had ever seen (and still is to this day).
  - I watched Lawrence of Arabia today for the first time, and I love this movie. To be honest, I was afraid it might be boring because it runs 3 hours and 42 minutes, but it wasn’t at all, it was an adventure.
    - it's amazing to consider the contrast of filming sand and desert scenes in the early 60s vs Dune / Dune II and CGI today.
4. Seven
5. Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind
6. Inception
7. Jaws

Raiders of the Lost Ark

Bladerunner 

Godfather 2 

Heat(95)

The 2 Towers 

Rocky 

Tombstone 

The list goes on
8. Dark City, but only the director's cut. The twist would hit like crazy

## 3. Fool's Paradise was bad. That's it.

**Score:** 266
**Body:** It's not abysmal or anything, it's just boring and a nothing burger of a movie. The only times I laughed was when Dean Norris came in, which is a weird coincidence since I watched Breaking Bad yesterday, and when Kate Beckinsale's character came in with cotton pads on her eyes when telling the protagonist she's divorcing him got a laugh out of me too. That's it. The only two things that I got a positive out of this movie.

I honestly feel bad for Charlie Day. I was pretty interested on how he would direct a movie, but alas.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He didn't just direct this dumpster fire, he also wrote it.
  - At least he didn’t act in it
2. It’s awful
  - Yeah surprised not more people are saying this. 

I turned it off 65% of the way through. I just couldn’t commit to the remaining 35%. 

As bad as Charlie’s character was,
Ken Jeong as essentially a lead man wasn’t working either. Just abysmal, really.
  - Especially considering how much work he put into it, painstakingly reworking the script until he thought it was perfect. 

Back when they were still doing the Always Sunny podcast he mentioned in one episode before the film had come out how a bunch of people, some who he considered close friends, had told him it wasn’t good or it wouldn’t do well or something along those lines. He acted like those people were just unsupportive assholes. 

Sometimes being a good friend means telling someone what they need to hear, not what they want to hear. Unfortunately, people aren’t always willing to heed advice they don’t want to hear but you have to at least try. Listening to those people would’ve saved Charlie Day a lot of heartache because I think he took the reception to Fool’s Paradise personally.
    - I love Charlie Day, regardless of this movie sucking. 


I couldn't get into it and turned it off about 30-45 minutes in. I know he's capable of more than what this movie showed, this just wasn't the million dollar idea. 


You're right, though. Good friends shouldn't lie just to be nice. I've learned a lot of the better friends I've had were supportive while also respectfully critical.
3. And he took *years* to complete it. I don't understand how that level of fussing would end up with such a lousy film, nobody could possibly enjoy.
  - Sometimes you get too close to a thing to see it for what it really is
4. Genuine question… How do movies this bad get casts like this? That’s almost a Tarantino/Scorsese/Nolan-level cast.
  - Connections. Charlie Day is well liked among other actors, it's his first time directing which will get lots of support, and he's friends with Guillermo which also brings some clout.

## 4. What's the worst headache you've ever received from watching a movie just by it's editing?

**Score:** 154
**Body:** For me it's Hardcore Henry. So thankful I only watched it at home and not a theater. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm glad I don't have this problem because I f... love this movie.

Edit: If you liked this movie, watch the video that inspired it (Bad motherf\*cker by Biting Elbows)

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rgox84KE7iY&list=RDRgox84KE7iY&start\_radio=1](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rgox84KE7iY&list=RDRgox84KE7iY&start_radio=1)
  - Yes it’s paced and edited well.
  - Very interesting flick at least technically, being filmed entirely in first-person, but some people do get severe motion sickness from these things. 

The poor fucks. 

Even in some video games, they have to have a consistent dot in the center of the screen or the option for it just to subdue that. 
2. FPS the movie....
3. Bohemian Rhapsody. How that film won best editing is beyond me.
  - Maybe the voters thought "best" also meant "most."
4. This was the first movie my wife and I saw in theaters. We both had such a fun time with it.
5. Cloverfield with the shaky camera
6. I didn’t know this movie existed. Thanks!
  - Oh you're gonna love it (or hate it) there's no between

## 5. Which of these three was your favorite?

**Score:** 142

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. OBAA
  - And it's not close
2. One Battle. Just on a whole other level
3. Have only seen Frankenstein. Quite Good. Interesting take on the old.
4. One battle 
5. 1. OBAA 
2. Frankenstein 
3. Sinners
6. Torn between 1 and 3.
7. OBAA by a country mile
8. *Sinners* was so good, I watched it one night and then again the next day. All 3 are good though. 

I found *Frankenstein* a bit sluggish in the middle. The last hour, with Jacob Elordi, was incredible and absolutely brought me back to attention.
9. Personally, Frankenstein.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Train Dreams and Eastern Philosophy

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I just finished this beautiful film, and although there’s plenty to discuss, I really wanted to hone in on the motif of intricacy and interconnectedness, especially as a method of dealing with profound grief.

Throughout the film, the forest serves as metaphor for the intricacy of life and the interconnected web of human experiences, and this seems to serve as a brief antidote to Grainier’s profound grief. His final epiphany seems to solidify the importance of recognising this truth, that when we can recognise the connection between all, this can provide a deep sense of meaning and serve as an important antidote to grief. In addition, his death, and the manner in which the natural world consumes his body, seems to reinforce this idea. 

I couldn’t help but view this epiphany through the lens of Eastern/Buddhist philosophy, especially the notion of non-duality and the importance of recognising that we are not separate self but rather an expansive part of the interconnected web of experience.

Anyway, I have no understanding of the context of the film, or it’s inspiration, and was wondering if these were some of the ideas the creators were toying with? 



### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The movie is based on a 2012 novella by Denis Johnson. Johnson was a heroin addict and alcoholic for parts of the 70s and 80s but eventually got sober and became a self described Christian.  I'm not sure what exactly his religious beliefs or philosophical musings were. I've only read one his shorty story collections (it's called Jesus' Son, but the name is a Velvet Underground/ heroin reference, not a religious one) which was quite interesting, but I didn't notice any strong Eastern influence. https://lareviewofbooks.org/article/books-of-revelation-christianity-and-miracles-in-the-life-and-work-of-denis-johnson/

I agree the movie was really lovely and moving. It's a good thoughtful picture
2. I think it might be more that Eastern/Buddhist philosophy taps into some universal truths that are also explored in this movie, rather than the filmmaker including some Buddhist/Eastern philosophies in this movie, if you see what I mean. It's a beautiful film.
  - Definitely, that’s what I though. The connection to all idea overlaps in most religions, some it’s probably some flavour of that notion.

## 2. Oppenheimer (2023): Actions have consequences

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Christopher Nolan's Oppenheimer is about many things. It's fascinating how much ground he covers within those 3 hours. After watching it you feel as if you saw a mini-series. It is about Oppenheimer the man, it is about the Manhatten Project, it is about the politics that surrounded Oppenheimer, it is about battle of egos among other things.

But finishing the film the one thing that lingers in your mind the most is the haunting final scene where Oppenheimer and Einstein have a chat about the terrible chain reaction his work has started. The final shot is where the camera gets close to Oppenheimer's face and we see how troubled he is by what he became part of.

This is something Oppenheimer feels throughout the second half of the film. Starting from when he gets news of the bombs being dropped on the Japanese cities. A change occurs within him and we see it in a masterful scene where he has to give a speech to people at Los Alamos after the Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

You could say it occurs slightly before when the bombs are being transported from Los Alamos and he stands there helpless as he nows he no longer has control over the situation.

This is a suprising change to the Oppenheimer we saw in the first half. This was a very passionate scientist and he happily jumps at the opportunity to head the Manhatten Project (although he plays it cool in front of Matt Damon's character). Throughout the first half he maintains that the work they are doing is for good. That it is a deterrent to prevent all future wars. 

The film shows that Oppenheimer was thinking short term. The end of WW2 is what was in mind. Of course he was in a race with the other countries as well. We see a couple of scenes where he convinces his colleagues about the benefit of the work they are doing. He is very sure of it all. But once the bombs went of he then realized exactly what he had been part of. To Truman he says he feels like he has got blood on his hands. And then the horrifying long term consequences come to his realization.

He also realizes that he was just a cog in the machine. At one moment the most important man perhaps in the US (after the President). The next moment he was a helpless bystander. And then he became a villain too. This leads into another theme of the film of how governments use and celebrate individuals for certain projects and chew them out the moment they are of no use.

All that is something Oppenheimer (the character in the film, not the real person) must have known but perhaps pushed it down in his brain to focus on other things. He used up all his passion and excitement for science for this project only to later realize that the work he was doing was never going to sit in a cupboard. That he was a fool for thinking so.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree that unintended consequences weigh heavily across the film. I think it puts Oppenheimer's arrogance on full display how he thinks his curiosity (scientific, political, and social) is self-evidently justified, but his understanding of the physical world does not translate to an understanding of how people work, and ultimately his inability to temper his indulgences spell his undoing, hurt those around him, and possibly doom the planet.


I read that there were plenty of great filmmakers that wanted to take a crack at American Prometheus but they just didn't know what to do with it, which makes Nolan's adaptation all the more impressive. I think the dueling narratives (Strauss black and white, Oppie and color) were a wonderful way to bring mutually-assured destruction to the story's forefront, and another key detail to me is when Oppie asks "Can't you see the revolution everywhere?" and is seen reading *The Wasteland*, but the text clearly went over his head. The bookends of the film show him staring at rain (presumably during the cruelest month) and only at the end does he seem to get it, and he shuts his eyes.


Edit: words are hard
2. I don't think he's ever in control of events, or even believes himself to be. When the government ask him to join the project, it's very much "we're doing this with or without you."

He's carried along by events throughout, and the inevitability of it all is a major theme. It's illustrated in the shot at the end where he's talking to Einstein, and Strauss is slowly walking towards them in the background.
  - Well he is in control of the Manhatten project. And he does so due to his desire to be there. But we do realize that in the end regrets being part of that whole thing.
3. I mean they were in a literal race against Nazis for the atomic bomb. Yes opening the atomic genie was bad bud Hitler getting the bomb would be much much worse. There were no great options at the time.

## 3. 'Eternity' is a sci-fi rom-com that trades logic for vibes (and it works)

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I just watched *Eternity* and honestly? It’s the comfort watch I needed.

I gave it a 3/5. It’s high-concept—treating the afterlife like a travel agency with "sold out" packages—but it refuses to take itself too seriously. Elizabeth Olsen is incredibly endearing, and the chemistry with both Callum Turner and Miles Teller works well to sell the central dilemma.

What I really liked was the subtext about "Perfection." The main character is trying to curate a perfect afterlife just like she tried to curate a perfect life, only to realize that the flaws are the point.

The plot definitely gets predictable halfway through, and the third act meanders, but the production design (the "Men-Free" zone is a great gag) and the cast make it a breeze.

Has anyone else seen it? Did you feel the ending worked?

Full review here: [https://amnesicreviews.substack.com/p/eternity-charmed-in-the-afterlife](https://amnesicreviews.substack.com/p/eternity-charmed-in-the-afterlife)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)
